message.content:
You are ONE of N parallel CANDIDATE planners (generate-N-then-judge) for plan-flow goal **G13**. Run in **CANDIDATE MODE**: ground yourself READ-ONLY against the actual repo and the goal's answered Q&A, then RETURN a full candidate task-DAG as a single fenced ```json block. Write NOTHING to any ledger. Emit no status token.

Goal **G13** (milestone M50, `goals` ledger) — "Operability & safety for weighted aggregation + pacing". Read its full description from the ledger. Its five clarifying questions Q51–Q55 are ALL ANSWERED — these answers are BINDING scope decisions:

- **Q51 (priority-class scope):** Item 3(ii)'s "latency/priority class" is scoped to protecting wanbond's OWN telemetry PROBE frames (`frame.KindProbe`) from pacer shedding under overload (so overload cannot induce spurious path-DOWN / failover flaps). Inner-tunnel ICMP prioritization is EXPLICITLY OUT OF SCOPE and must be documented as architecturally infeasible (the WG tunnel payload is opaque `ClassData` to the pacer). ClassControl (WG handshake/keepalive) is already pacing-exempt (D22) — do not re-plan that.
- **Q52 (capacity-sanity guard):** When `policy="weighted"` is selected: if `link_bandwidth` IS declared AND `EngageFraction × per_path_capacity_fps` exceeds the implied line-rate capacity (aggregation mathematically can never engage), that is a HARD config-load FAILURE (fail-fast, refuse to start, actionable error). If `link_bandwidth` is NOT declared, it is a startup WARN only (log + a `wanbond_weighted_capacity_sane` gauge=0), never blocks startup. Use `SizePacingFromBDP`/BDP math for the implied capacity (impliedCapacityFPS = link_bandwidth/(8×avgWireFrameBytes)).
- **Q53 (G2 boundary — Option A):** G13 stays OPERABILITY-ONLY: the capacity-sanity WARN/FAIL guard (item 2), the `wanbond_aggregation_engaged` + offered-load/threshold observability (item 1), and the PROBE priority class (item 3(ii)). The actual `per_path_capacity_fps` AUTO-DERIVE from `link_bandwidth` AND the item-3(a) BDP-sizing DOCS remain G2/Q20's scope — G13 does NOT own them (reference, do not restate). Note: G2's Q20 pacing-sizing is intent-only (M14/M16/M17 empty) but you must NOT fold it into G13.
- **Q54 (metric shape):** Per-peer labels (`labelPeer`, consistent with existing FEC/resequencer per-peer series). Expose: `wanbond_aggregation_engaged{peer}` (bool gauge), `wanbond_offered_load_fps{peer}` gauge, and static `wanbond_aggregation_engage_threshold_fps{peer}` / `wanbond_aggregation_disengage_threshold_fps{peer}` gauges. ALSO emit an INFO engage↔disengage transition log line (parity with liveness up/down transitions). The scheduler already holds this state (`s.aggregating`, `loadRate` EWMA, EngageFraction/DisengageFraction × PerPathCapacity) but exposes none to the metrics Source/Collector.
- **Q55 (acceptance harness):** ALL per-task acceptance is via the `-tags e2e` netns fixture for uniformity (privileged, `just e2e`, end-to-end) — NOT unprivileged unit tests. Note: TestFixtureImpairment (test/e2e/fixture_impairment_test.go, T35) exposes rateMbit/lossPct capping and metrics.Fetch scrapes /metrics; if it cannot yet drive a SUSTAINED overload that flips `wanbond_aggregation_engaged` AND concurrently observe PROBE-frame starvation, an explicit fixture-EXTENSION task must be an up-front dependency of the tasks that need it.

Definition-of-done for the repo (do NOT re-derive): `go test` GREEN plus `just lint` across default+e2e+realhosts tags (every task's gate includes lint). Keep README.md / docs/design.md / docs/install.md in sync where a task changes operator-visible behavior (per AGENTS.md).

Ground against the real code: internal/sched (weighted scheduler, FrameClass, token bucket, aggregation gate state), internal/metrics (Source/Collector, labelPeer/labelPath, Exposition), internal/config (validate, per_path_capacity_fps default 10000, link_bandwidth, SizePacingFromBDP), internal/frame (KindProbe), internal/telemetry (liveness transitions, Prober), test/e2e (netns fixture, TestFixtureImpairment).

Produce a candidate plan whose tasks are fine-grained, correctly sequenced (dependsOn edges), each with a crisp testable `-tags e2e` acceptance criterion, grounded in real files, and COMPLETE for the answered scope (items 1, 2, 3(ii) + docs of the on/off tradeoff and the inner-ICMP infeasibility note; NOT the G2-owned auto-derive/BDP-sizing docs). Return EXACTLY this shape and nothing else:

```json
{
  "milestones": [{ "title": "...", "dependsOn": ["<other milestone title>"] }],
  "tasks": [{ "headline": "...", "description": "...", "acceptance": "...", "suggestedModel": "...", "milestone": "<milestone title>", "dependsOn": ["<other task headline>"], "ledgerRefs": ["goals:G13"] }],
  "rationale": "..."
}
```

End your reply with a `### Session summary` section (role: candidate planner (opus), goal G13, "candidate plan emitted", milestone/task counts).

attachment.content:
- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.
- cq:plan: Start a plan-flow goal — create the goal (from free text OR one-or-more idea-ids, one goal per idea), then hand off to the planner for the first clarifying questions.
- cq:plan-review: Shared adversarial plan-review rubric — judge an emitted plan for a goal against the fine-grained / sequenced / testable / grounded / complete rubric, classify findings into new_questions / criticism / defects, and emit a single fenced-json verdict on stdout. The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.
- cq:plan:follow-up: Add new scope to an EXISTING plan-flow goal — append the follow-up request, re-open the goal, and hand to the planner for a fresh clarifying round.
- cq:planners: Session-only planner-set override — parse a natural-language planner instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_planners/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- cq:reviewers: Session-only reviewer-set override — parse a natural-language reviewer instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_reviewers/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

```go
1	package config

13	
14	// requiredMode is the exact permission bits the config file must carry. The file
15	// holds the WireGuard private key and the outer-control PSK, so it must not be
16	// readable by group or others.
17	const requiredMode fs.FileMode = 0o600
18	
19	// Load reads, validates, and returns the configuration at path. It fails fast:
20	// the file must have mode 0600 exactly, must be valid TOML, and must satisfy the
21	// required-field invariants.
22	func Load(path string) (*Config, error) {
23		info, err := os.Stat(path)
24		if err != nil {
25			return nil, fmt.Errorf("config: %w", err)
26		}
27		if perm := info.Mode().Perm(); perm != requiredMode {
28			return nil, fmt.Errorf("config %s: insecure permissions %#o, must be %#o (holds private key and PSK)", path, perm, requiredMode)
29		}
30	
31		data, err := os.ReadFile(path)
32		if err != nil {
33			return nil, fmt.Errorf("config: %w", err)
34		}
35	
36		var c Config
37		dec := toml.NewDecoder(bytes.NewReader(data)).DisallowUnknownFields()
38		if err := dec.Decode(&c); err != nil {
39			var strictErr *toml.StrictMissingError
40			if errors.As(err, &strictErr) {
41				return nil, fmt.Errorf("config %s: unknown key %s", path, unknownKeys(strictErr))
42			}
43			return nil, fmt.Errorf("config %s: %w", path, err)
44		}
45		if err := c.normalize(); err != nil {
46			return nil, fmt.Errorf("config %s: %w", path, err)
47		}
48		if err := c.validate(); err != nil {
49			return nil, fmt.Errorf("config %s: %w", path, err)
50		}
51		return &c, nil
52	}
53	
54	// unknownKeys renders a StrictMissingError's row list as a comma-separated
55	// list of dotted key paths (e.g. "wireguard.peers.nane"), so a misspelled
56	// TOML key surfaces as a precise, single-line diagnostic instead of the
57	// library's raw multiline dump.
58	func unknownKeys(err *toml.StrictMissingError) string {
59		keys := make([]string, 0, len(err.Errors))
60		for _, e := range err.Errors {
61			keys = append(keys, strings.Join(e.Key(), "."))
62		}
63		return strings.Join(keys, ", ")
64	}
```

```go
375	// subtracts the total weight from the winner. Over many calls the selection frequency
376	// of each path converges to its weight share, interleaved smoothly rather than
377	// bursted. Caller holds s.mu; weights is aligned to eligible order.
378	func (s *WeightedScheduler) swrrPickLocked(eligible []int, weights []float64) int {
379		var total float64
380		for _, w := range weights {
381			total += w
382		}
383		best := -1
384		var bestCur float64
385		for k, gi := range eligible {
386			s.current[gi] += weights[k]
387			if best < 0 || s.current[gi] > bestCur {
388				best = gi
389				bestCur = s.current[gi]
390			}
391		}
392		s.current[best] -= total
393		return best
394	}
```

```go
26	// WireGuard control frames (handshake init/response, cookie reply, keepalive) a
27	// different admission policy than bulk data. Pick() has no other frame-type
28	// visibility, so this is the ONLY channel by which frame-type reaches the pacer.
29	type FrameClass uint8
30	
31	const (
32		// ClassData is bulk/opaque data (a WireGuard transport datagram carrying tunnelled
33		// payload). It is fully paced: under a weighted scheduler with pacing enabled it is
34		// subject to the per-path token buckets and is shed (PickPaced) when they are empty.
35		ClassData FrameClass = iota
36		// ClassControl is a WireGuard control frame — handshake initiation/response, cookie
37		// reply, or keepalive. It is pacing-EXEMPT: a pacing scheduler never sheds it for an
38		// empty token bucket and never spends a data token for it, so sustained bulk overload
39		// cannot starve rekey (defect D22). Control frames originate from OUR OWN WireGuard
40		// engine on the send path (an attacker cannot inject them here), and WireGuard's own
41		// state machine bounds their rate, so exempting them from the data pace is safe.
42		ClassControl
43	)
44	
45	// Scheduler is the send-side path-selection policy the multipath Bind consults
46	// for every outbound datagram. It is the extension seam for the send scheduler:
47	// active-backup failover (ActiveBackup, the P1 MVP) is one implementation; the
48	// later weighted / FEC-aware policy (T21) is a DIFFERENT implementation of this
49	// same interface, swapped in at the composition root with no Bind change.
50	type Scheduler interface {
51		// Pick returns the index of the path the next datagram must egress on, in
52		// the path priority order the scheduler was built over (index 0 = preferred
53		// primary). It returns a negative value when no datagram should be sent this
54		// call; the negative value is one of the Pick* sentinels (PickNone for no
55		// eligible path, PickPaced for a healthy-but-paced-out shed), so the caller can
56		// tell a genuine outage apart from deliberate rate limiting. Pick is safe for
57		// concurrent callers on the Bind's send path.
58		//
59		// class is the frame's traffic class (defect D22): a pacing scheduler exempts
60		// ClassControl (WireGuard handshake/keepalive) from the data token buckets so
61		// bulk overload cannot shed control frames and starve rekey, while ClassData is
62		// fully paced. A non-pacing scheduler (active-backup) ignores class.
63		//
64		// Pick MAY be stateful: a weighted/aggregating scheduler advances its
65		// distribution bookkeeping (deficit/round-robin credits, pacing tokens, offered-
66		// load meter) on every call, so ONE Pick consumes ONE frame-selection slot.
67		// Callers that only want the liveness-derived active set refreshed — without
68		// perturbing distribution — MUST call Recompute, not Pick (see the T40 eager-
69		// failover nudge in the multipath Bind).
70		Pick(class FrameClass) int
71	
72		// Recompute refreshes the scheduler's liveness-derived selection state (which
73		// paths are eligible, which is the active primary) from the current PathHealth,
74		// and logs any active-path transition, WITHOUT selecting or consuming a frame
75		// slot: it advances no distribution/pacing/load state. It is the split-out
76		// "recompute the eligible/active set from liveness" half of the old Pick, so the
77		// eager-failover nudge (defect D18/T40) can drive an egress-lull failover recompute
78		// without stealing a weighted-distribution slot the way a spurious Pick would. For
79		// an idempotent scheduler (active-backup) it is exactly Pick without the return;
80		// for a stateful one (weighted) it is strictly the non-consuming subset. It is
81		// safe for concurrent callers and never calls back into the Bind.
82		Recompute()
83	}
84

86	// to derive distribution weights: the read side of the T13 telemetry Estimate
```

```go
54	// byteSample is the previous scrape's cumulative (tx+rx) total for one (peer,path) and
55	// the instant it was taken, so the next scrape can divide the delta by the elapsed time.

57		total   uint64
58		atNanos int64
59	}

61	// newMetricsSource builds a metrics.Source over the Bind (per-peer path traffic, FEC,
62	// and resequencer counters) and the engine session seam (WG-session snapshot). The
63	// clock is injected so the throughput derivation is deterministic under test.
64	func newMetricsSource(provider trafficProvider, session sessionSnapshotter, clock telemetry.Clock) *metricsSource {
65		return &metricsSource{provider: provider, session: session, clock: clock, last: make(map[sampleKey]byteSample)}
66	}

68	// Paths implements metrics.Source. It is called on the scrape goroutine (the /metrics
69	// HTTP handler), which the prometheus registry may run concurrently, so the per-
70	// (peer,path) last-sample map is guarded. The underlying provider.PeerSnapshots() reads
71	// atomics and each prober's synchronized Estimate without holding the Bind's send lock
72	// across a prober call, so scraping never blocks Send.
73	func (s *metricsSource) Paths() []metrics.PathSnapshot {
74		peers := s.provider.PeerSnapshots()
75		nowNanos := s.clock.Now().UnixNano()
76	
77		total := 0
78		for _, peer := range peers {
79			total += len(peer.Paths)
80		}
81	
82		s.mu.Lock()
83		defer s.mu.Unlock()
84		out := make([]metrics.PathSnapshot, 0, total)
85		for _, peer := range peers {
86			for _, t := range peer.Paths {
87				key := sampleKey{peer: peer.Name, path: t.Name}
88				byteTotal := t.TxBytes + t.RxBytes
89				var tput float64
90				if prev, ok := s.last[key]; ok {
91					dtSeconds := float64(nowNanos-prev.atNanos) / 1e9
92					if dtSeconds > 0 && byteTotal >= prev.total {
93						tput = float64(byteTotal-prev.total) * 8 / dtSeconds
94					}
95				}
96				s.last[key] = byteSample{total: byteTotal, atNanos: nowNanos}
97				out = append(out, metrics.PathSnapshot{
98					Peer:                    peer.Name,
99					Name:                    t.Name,
100					TxBytes:                 t.TxBytes,
101					RxBytes:                 t.RxBytes,
102					ThroughputBitsPerSecond: tput,
103					Estimate:                t.Estimate,
104					State:                   t.State,
105				})
106			}
107		}
108		return out
109	}

113	// per-path throughput, the FEC counters are cumulative and need no rate derivation or
114	// prior-sample state, so this is a direct pass-through of the Bind's lock-free snapshot.

116		peers := s.provider.PeerSnapshots()

122				DataPackets:          f.DataFrames,

126				DataBytes:            f.DataBytes,
127				RepairBytes:          f.ParityBytes,
128				ResidualLossRatio:    f.ResidualLoss,

142		}
```

```go
14	// Exposition is a parsed /metrics scrape: the metric families keyed by name. It
15	// exists so e2e assertions can look up a per-path series value without each test
16	// re-implementing the Prometheus text-format parser.
17	type Exposition struct {
18		families map[string]*dto.MetricFamily
19	}
20	
21	// Fetch GETs baseURL (e.g. "http://127.0.0.1:9095/metrics") with client and
22	// parses the response into an Exposition. It is the scrape helper future e2e
23	// tests use to drive assertions against a running endpoint.
24	func Fetch(ctx context.Context, client *http.Client, baseURL string) (Exposition, error) {
25		req, err := http.NewRequestWithContext(ctx, http.MethodGet, baseURL, nil)
26		if err != nil {
27			return Exposition{}, fmt.Errorf("metrics: build scrape request: %w", err)
28		}
29		resp, err := client.Do(req)
30		if err != nil {
31			return Exposition{}, fmt.Errorf("metrics: scrape %q: %w", baseURL, err)
32		}
33		defer func() { _ = resp.Body.Close() }()
34		if resp.StatusCode != http.StatusOK {
35			return Exposition{}, fmt.Errorf("metrics: scrape %q: status %s", baseURL, resp.Status)
36		}
37	
38		// v0.66's TextParser requires an explicit name-validation scheme; the
39		// zero-value parser leaves it unset and panics. UTF8Validation matches the
40		// promhttp exposition this endpoint serves.
41		parser := expfmt.NewTextParser(model.UTF8Validation)
42		families, err := parser.TextToMetricFamilies(resp.Body)
43		if err != nil {
44			return Exposition{}, fmt.Errorf("metrics: parse exposition: %w", err)
45		}
46		// Drain any residue so the connection can be reused.
47		_, _ = io.Copy(io.Discard, resp.Body)
48		return Exposition{families: families}, nil
49	}
50	
51	// Families returns the parsed metric families keyed by metric name.
52	func (e Exposition) Families() map[string]*dto.MetricFamily { return e.families }
53	
54	// Has reports whether the named metric family is present (registered), regardless
55	// of its value — useful for asserting the placeholder FEC series exist.
56	func (e Exposition) Has(name string) bool {
57		_, ok := e.families[name]
58		return ok
59	}
60

62	// label, and whether such a series was found. It reads whichever of the
63	// gauge/counter value fields the sample carries.

65		return e.labeledValue(name, labelPath, path)
66	}

72	}

74	// PeerPathValue returns the value of a per-(peer,path) series `name` (T94) for the
75	// given peer+path label pair, and whether such a series was found.
76	func (e Exposition) PeerPathValue(name, peer, path string) (float64, bool) {
77		fam, ok := e.families[name]
78		if !ok {
79			return 0, false
80		}
81		for _, m := range fam.GetMetric() {
82			var gotPeer, gotPath string
83			var havePeer, havePath bool
84			for _, lp := range m.GetLabel() {
85				switch lp.GetName() {
86				case labelPeer:
87					gotPeer, havePeer = lp.GetValue(), true
88				case labelPath:
89					gotPath, havePath = lp.GetValue(), true
90				}
91			}
92			if havePeer && havePath && gotPeer == peer && gotPath == path {
93				return metricValue(m), true
94			}
95		}
96		return 0, false
97	}
98	
99	// Value returns the value of an unlabeled series `name` (e.g. the FEC
100	// placeholders), and whether it was found.
101	func (e Exposition) Value(name string) (float64, bool) {
102		fam, ok := e.families[name]
103		if !ok {
104			return 0, false
105		}
106		for _, m := range fam.GetMetric() {
107			if len(m.GetLabel()) == 0 {
108				return metricValue(m), true
109			}
110		}
111		return 0, false
112	}
113	
114	func (e Exposition) labeledValue(name, labelName, labelValue string) (float64, bool) {
115		fam, ok := e.families[name]
116		if !ok {
117			return 0, false
118		}
119		for _, m := range fam.GetMetric() {
120			for _, lp := range m.GetLabel() {
121				if lp.GetName() == labelName && lp.GetValue() == labelValue {
122					return metricValue(m), true
123				}
124			}
125		}
126		return 0, false
127	}
128	
129	// metricValue extracts the scalar value from a dto.Metric, reading whichever of
130	// the gauge/counter/untyped fields is populated.
131	func metricValue(m *dto.Metric) float64 {
132		switch {
133		case m.GetGauge() != nil:
134			return m.GetGauge().GetValue()
135		case m.GetCounter() != nil:
136			return m.GetCounter().GetValue()
137		case m.GetUntyped() != nil:
138			return m.GetUntyped().GetValue()
139		default:
140			return 0
141		}
142	}
```

```go
25		req, err := http.NewRequestWithContext(ctx, http.MethodGet, baseURL, nil)
26		if err != nil {
27			return Exposition{}, fmt.Errorf("metrics: build scrape request: %w", err)
28		}
29		resp, err := client.Do(req)
30		if err != nil {
31			return Exposition{}, fmt.Errorf("metrics: scrape %q: %w", baseURL, err)
32		}
33		defer func() { _ = resp.Body.Close() }()
34		if resp.StatusCode != http.StatusOK {
35			return Exposition{}, fmt.Errorf("metrics: scrape %q: status %s", baseURL, resp.Status)
36		}
37	
38		// v0.66's TextParser requires an explicit name-validation scheme; the
39		// zero-value parser leaves it unset and panics. UTF8Validation matches the
40		// promhttp exposition this endpoint serves.
41		parser := expfmt.NewTextParser(model.UTF8Validation)
42		families, err := parser.TextToMetricFamilies(resp.Body)
43		if err != nil {
44			return Exposition{}, fmt.Errorf("metrics: parse exposition: %w", err)
45		}
46		// Drain any residue so the connection can be reused.
47		_, _ = io.Copy(io.Discard, resp.Body)
48		return Exposition{families: families}, nil
49	}

51	// Families returns the parsed metric families keyed by metric name.
52	func (e Exposition) Families() map[string]*dto.MetricFamily { return e.families }
53	
54	// Has reports whether the named metric family is present (registered), regardless
55	// of its value — useful for asserting the placeholder FEC series exist.
56	func (e Exposition) Has(name string) bool {
57		_, ok := e.families[name]
58		return ok
59	}

62	// label, and whether such a series was found. It reads whichever of the

66	}

69	// the given `peer` label value, and whether such a series was found.
70	func (e Exposition) PeerValue(name, peer string) (float64, bool) {
71		return e.labeledValue(name, labelPeer, peer)
72	}
73	
74	// PeerPathValue returns the value of a per-(peer,path) series `name` (T94) for the
75	// given peer+path label pair, and whether such a series was found.
76	func (e Exposition) PeerPathValue(name, peer, path string) (float64, bool) {
77		fam, ok := e.families[name]
78		if !ok {
79			return 0, false
80		}
81		for _, m := range fam.GetMetric() {
82			var gotPeer, gotPath string
83			var havePeer, havePath bool
84			for _, lp := range m.GetLabel() {
85				switch lp.GetName() {
86				case labelPeer:
87					gotPeer, havePeer = lp.GetValue(), true
88				case labelPath:
89					gotPath, havePath = lp.GetValue(), true
90				}
91			}
92			if havePeer && havePath && gotPeer == peer && gotPath == path {
93				return metricValue(m), true
94			}
95		}
96		return 0, false
97	}
98	
99	// Value returns the value of an unlabeled series `name` (e.g. the FEC
100	// placeholders), and whether it was found.
101	func (e Exposition) Value(name string) (float64, bool) {
102		fam, ok := e.families[name]
103		if !ok {
104			return 0, false
105		}
106		for _, m := range fam.GetMetric() {
107			if len(m.GetLabel()) == 0 {
108				return metricValue(m), true
109			}
110		}
111		return 0, false
112	}
113	
114	func (e Exposition) labeledValue(name, labelName, labelValue string) (float64, bool) {
115		fam, ok := e.families[name]
116		if !ok {
117			return 0, false
118		}
119		for _, m := range fam.GetMetric() {
120			for _, lp := range m.GetLabel() {
121				if lp.GetName() == labelName && lp.GetValue() == labelValue {
122					return metricValue(m), true
123				}
124			}
125		}
126		return 0, false
127	}

129	// metricValue extracts the scalar value from a dto.Metric, reading whichever of
130	// the gauge/counter/untyped fields is populated.
131	func metricValue(m *dto.Metric) float64 {
132		switch {
133		case m.GetGauge() != nil:
134			return m.GetGauge().GetValue()
135		case m.GetCounter() != nil:
136			return m.GetCounter().GetValue()
137		case m.GetUntyped() != nil:
138			return m.GetUntyped().GetValue()
139		default:
140			return 0
141		}
142	}
```

```go
183	// NewWeighted builds the weighted-aggregation scheduler over parallel health and
184	// quality slices in priority order (index 0 = preferred primary). health and quality
185	// must be the same length; a quality element MAY be nil (that path then gets a
186	// neutral weight until a real PathQuality is wired). It fails fast on an empty or
187	// length-mismatched set, a nil health element, a nil dependency, or an invalid config.
188	func NewWeighted(health []PathHealth, quality []PathQuality, cfg WeightedConfig, clock telemetry.Clock, logger log.Logger) (*WeightedScheduler, error) {
189		if len(health) == 0 {
190			return nil, fmt.Errorf("sched: at least one path health source is required")
191		}
192		if len(quality) != len(health) {
193			return nil, fmt.Errorf("sched: quality set (%d) must be one entry per health source (%d)", len(quality), len(health))
194		}
195		for i, h := range health {
196			if h == nil {
197				return nil, fmt.Errorf("sched: path %d health source is nil", i)
198			}
199		}
200		if clock == nil {
201			return nil, fmt.Errorf("sched: clock is required")
202		}
203		if logger == nil {
204			return nil, fmt.Errorf("sched: logger is required")
205		}
206		if err := cfg.validate(); err != nil {
207			return nil, err
208		}
209		return &WeightedScheduler{
210			health:  append([]PathHealth(nil), health...),
211			quality: append([]PathQuality(nil), quality...),
212			clock:   clock,
213			cfg:     cfg,
214			log:     logger.Component("sched"),
215			current: make([]float64, len(health)),
216			tokens:  fullBuckets(len(health), cfg.PacingBurst),
217			active:  -1,
218		}, nil
219	}

221	// SetFEC installs the P3+ redundancy policy (nil clears it). It is the documented
222	// extension point for FEC-aware path selection; the current single-path Pick does
223	// not consult it (redundant transmission needs the P3 Bind fan-out), but
224	// RedundantPaths exposes it so a P3 Bind can query the additional paths per frame.
225	func (s *WeightedScheduler) SetFEC(fec FECPolicy) {
226		s.mu.Lock()
227		defer s.mu.Unlock()
228		s.fec = fec
229	}

231	// RedundantPaths reports the ADDITIONAL path indices a frame scheduled onto chosen
232	// should also egress on, per the installed FECPolicy over the current eligible set.
233	// It returns nil when no policy is installed (no FEC). It is the P3 hook a redundant-
234	// transmission Bind would call alongside Pick; today it is a documented, unit-tested
235	// seam with no in-tree caller on the hot path.
236	func (s *WeightedScheduler) RedundantPaths(chosen int) []int {
237		s.mu.Lock()
238		defer s.mu.Unlock()
239		if s.fec == nil {
240			return nil
241		}
242		return s.fec.RedundantPaths(chosen, s.eligibleLocked())
243	}

245	// Pick selects the path for the NEXT frame and advances all per-frame distribution
246	// state (offered-load meter, aggregation gate, pacing buckets, round-robin credits).
247	// It returns a negative value when no path is eligible OR when every eligible path
248	// is paced out (the frame is dropped rather than queued — pacing bounds egress and,
249	// by dropping the overflow, bounds the send backlog). It is safe for concurrent
250	// callers.
251	func (s *WeightedScheduler) Pick(class FrameClass) int {
252		now := s.clock.Now()
253		s.mu.Lock()
254		defer s.mu.Unlock()
255	
256		// Capture the inter-sample idle gap BEFORE observeLoadLocked advances lastLoad: a
257		// long gap since the previous offered frame is itself the strongest evidence of low
258		// load, and the collapse hysteresis must count it (criticism #1 — otherwise an
259		// abrupt overload stop leaves the gate engaged across the whole idle span and the
260		// next low burst dribbles onto the metered path).
261		gap := s.loadGapLocked(now)
262		s.recomputeLocked(now)       // liveness refresh + eager-failover log
263		s.observeLoadLocked(now)     // this Pick is one offered frame (decays across the gap)
264		s.updateGateLocked(now, gap) // engage/disengage aggregation (hysteresis, idle-aware)
265		s.refillLocked(now)          // top up pacing buckets
266	
267		eligible := s.eligibleLocked()
268		if len(eligible) == 0 {
269			return PickNone
270		}
271		if !s.aggregating {
272			// Data-thrift: at low load only the primary (best eligible) carries traffic;
273			// the metered backup stays idle. A path-down excludes it from eligible, so
274			// eligible[0] is the surviving highest-priority path — failover is preserved.
275			return s.serveLocked(now, eligible[0], class)
276		}
277		weights := s.weightsLocked(eligible)
278		return s.selectAggregatingLocked(now, eligible, weights, class)
279	}

281	// loadGapLocked returns the wall-clock gap since the previous offered-load sample, or
282	// 0 before the first sample. Caller holds s.mu.
283	func (s *WeightedScheduler) loadGapLocked(now time.Time) time.Duration {
284		if !s.haveLoad {
285			return 0
286		}
287		gap := now.Sub(s.lastLoad)
288		if gap < 0 {
289			gap = 0
290		}
291		return gap
292	}

294	// Recompute refreshes only the liveness-derived eligible/active set from the current
295	// PathHealth and logs any active-path transition. It advances NONE of the per-frame
296	// distribution state (load meter, gate, pacing, round-robin credits), so the eager-
297	// failover nudge (defect D18/T40) drives an egress-lull failover recompute through it
298	// WITHOUT stealing a weighted-distribution slot the way a spurious Pick would. It is
299	// safe for concurrent callers.
300	func (s *WeightedScheduler) Recompute() {
301		now := s.clock.Now()
302		s.mu.Lock()
303		defer s.mu.Unlock()
304		s.recomputeLocked(now)
305	}

308	// transition on change. Caller holds s.mu.

310		best := s.bestEligibleLocked()

312	}

314	// serveLocked applies pacing to a single chosen path: it returns idx when the frame
315	// is admitted, or PickPaced when pacing is on and the path has no token (the frame is
316	// shed — dropped, bounding egress and backlog — and the shedding diagnostic is
317	// rate-limited). A ClassControl frame is pacing-EXEMPT (defect D22): it egresses on
318	// idx unconditionally, spending no token and never shed, so bulk overload cannot
319	// starve WireGuard rekey. Caller holds s.mu.
320	func (s *WeightedScheduler) serveLocked(now time.Time, idx int, class FrameClass) int {
321		if class == ClassControl {
322			return idx
323		}
324		if s.tryConsumeLocked(idx) {
325			return idx
326		}
327		return s.shedLocked(now)
328	}
```

```go
14	// requiredMode is the exact permission bits the config file must carry. The file
15	// holds the WireGuard private key and the outer-control PSK, so it must not be
16	// readable by group or others.
17	const requiredMode fs.FileMode = 0o600
18	
19	// Load reads, validates, and returns the configuration at path. It fails fast:
20	// the file must have mode 0600 exactly, must be valid TOML, and must satisfy the
21	// required-field invariants.
22	func Load(path string) (*Config, error) {
23		info, err := os.Stat(path)
24		if err != nil {
25			return nil, fmt.Errorf("config: %w", err)
26		}
27		if perm := info.Mode().Perm(); perm != requiredMode {
28			return nil, fmt.Errorf("config %s: insecure permissions %#o, must be %#o (holds private key and PSK)", path, perm, requiredMode)
29		}
30	
31		data, err := os.ReadFile(path)
32		if err != nil {
33			return nil, fmt.Errorf("config: %w", err)
34		}
35	
36		var c Config
37		dec := toml.NewDecoder(bytes.NewReader(data)).DisallowUnknownFields()
38		if err := dec.Decode(&c); err != nil {
39			var strictErr *toml.StrictMissingError
40			if errors.As(err, &strictErr) {
41				return nil, fmt.Errorf("config %s: unknown key %s", path, unknownKeys(strictErr))
42			}
43			return nil, fmt.Errorf("config %s: %w", path, err)
44		}
45		if err := c.normalize(); err != nil {
46			return nil, fmt.Errorf("config %s: %w", path, err)
47		}
48		if err := c.validate(); err != nil {
49			return nil, fmt.Errorf("config %s: %w", path, err)
50		}
51		return &c, nil
52	}
```

```go
183	// NewWeighted builds the weighted-aggregation scheduler over parallel health and
184	// quality slices in priority order (index 0 = preferred primary). health and quality
185	// must be the same length; a quality element MAY be nil (that path then gets a

188	func NewWeighted(health []PathHealth, quality []PathQuality, cfg WeightedConfig, clock telemetry.Clock, logger log.Logger) (*WeightedScheduler, error) {
189		if len(health) == 0 {
190			return nil, fmt.Errorf("sched: at least one path health source is required")
191		}
192		if len(quality) != len(health) {
193			return nil, fmt.Errorf("sched: quality set (%d) must be one entry per health source (%d)", len(quality), len(health))

211			quality: append([]PathQuality(nil), quality...),
212			clock:   clock,
213			cfg:     cfg,
214			log:     logger.Component("sched"),

221	// SetFEC installs the P3+ redundancy policy (nil clears it). It is the documented
222	// extension point for FEC-aware path selection; the current single-path Pick does
223	// not consult it (redundant transmission needs the P3 Bind fan-out), but
224	// RedundantPaths exposes it so a P3 Bind can query the additional paths per frame.
225	func (s *WeightedScheduler) SetFEC(fec FECPolicy) {
226		s.mu.Lock()
227		defer s.mu.Unlock()
228		s.fec = fec
229	}

231	// RedundantPaths reports the ADDITIONAL path indices a frame scheduled onto chosen
232	// should also egress on, per the installed FECPolicy over the current eligible set.
233	// It returns nil when no policy is installed (no FEC). It is the P3 hook a redundant-
234	// transmission Bind would call alongside Pick; today it is a documented, unit-tested
235	// seam with no in-tree caller on the hot path.
236	func (s *WeightedScheduler) RedundantPaths(chosen int) []int {
237		s.mu.Lock()
238		defer s.mu.Unlock()

245	// Pick selects the path for the NEXT frame and advances all per-frame distribution
246	// state (offered-load meter, aggregation gate, pacing buckets, round-robin credits).
247	// It returns a negative value when no path is eligible OR when every eligible path
248	// is paced out (the frame is dropped rather than queued — pacing bounds egress and,
249	// by dropping the overflow, bounds the send backlog). It is safe for concurrent
250	// callers.
251	func (s *WeightedScheduler) Pick(class FrameClass) int {
252		now := s.clock.Now()
253		s.mu.Lock()
254		defer s.mu.Unlock()
255	
256		// Capture the inter-sample idle gap BEFORE observeLoadLocked advances lastLoad: a
257		// long gap since the previous offered frame is itself the strongest evidence of low
258		// load, and the collapse hysteresis must count it (criticism #1 — otherwise an
259		// abrupt overload stop leaves the gate engaged across the whole idle span and the
260		// next low burst dribbles onto the metered path).
261		gap := s.loadGapLocked(now)
262		s.recomputeLocked(now)       // liveness refresh + eager-failover log
263		s.observeLoadLocked(now)     // this Pick is one offered frame (decays across the gap)
264		s.updateGateLocked(now, gap) // engage/disengage aggregation (hysteresis, idle-aware)
265		s.refillLocked(now)          // top up pacing buckets
266	
267		eligible := s.eligibleLocked()
268		if len(eligible) == 0 {
269			return PickNone
270		}
271		if !s.aggregating {
272			// Data-thrift: at low load only the primary (best eligible) carries traffic;
273			// the metered backup stays idle. A path-down excludes it from eligible, so
274			// eligible[0] is the surviving highest-priority path — failover is preserved.
275			return s.serveLocked(now, eligible[0], class)
276		}
277		weights := s.weightsLocked(eligible)
278		return s.selectAggregatingLocked(now, eligible, weights, class)
279	}

282	// 0 before the first sample. Caller holds s.mu.

284		if !s.haveLoad {

286		}
287		gap := now.Sub(s.lastLoad)
288		if gap < 0 {

295	// PathHealth and logs any active-path transition. It advances NONE of the per-frame

299	// safe for concurrent callers.

303		defer s.mu.Unlock()
304		s.recomputeLocked(now)

308	// transition on change. Caller holds s.mu.

310		best := s.bestEligibleLocked()

312	}

315	// is admitted, or PickPaced when pacing is on and the path has no token (the frame is
316	// shed — dropped, bounding egress and backlog — and the shedding diagnostic is
317	// rate-limited). A ClassControl frame is pacing-EXEMPT (defect D22): it egresses on
318	// idx unconditionally, spending no token and never shed, so bulk overload cannot
319	// starve WireGuard rekey. Caller holds s.mu.
320	func (s *WeightedScheduler) serveLocked(now time.Time, idx int, class FrameClass) int {
321		if class == ClassControl {
322			return idx
323		}
324		if s.tryConsumeLocked(idx) {
325			return idx
326		}
327		return s.shedLocked(now)
328	}
```

```go
14	// requiredMode is the exact permission bits the config file must carry. The file
15	// holds the WireGuard private key and the outer-control PSK, so it must not be
16	// readable by group or others.
17	const requiredMode fs.FileMode = 0o600
18	
19	// Load reads, validates, and returns the configuration at path. It fails fast:
20	// the file must have mode 0600 exactly, must be valid TOML, and must satisfy the
21	// required-field invariants.
22	func Load(path string) (*Config, error) {
23		info, err := os.Stat(path)
24		if err != nil {
25			return nil, fmt.Errorf("config: %w", err)
26		}
27		if perm := info.Mode().Perm(); perm != requiredMode {
28			return nil, fmt.Errorf("config %s: insecure permissions %#o, must be %#o (holds private key and PSK)", path, perm, requiredMode)
29		}
30	
31		data, err := os.ReadFile(path)
32		if err != nil {
33			return nil, fmt.Errorf("config: %w", err)
34		}
35	
36		var c Config
37		dec := toml.NewDecoder(bytes.NewReader(data)).DisallowUnknownFields()
38		if err := dec.Decode(&c); err != nil {
39			var strictErr *toml.StrictMissingError
40			if errors.As(err, &strictErr) {
41				return nil, fmt.Errorf("config %s: unknown key %s", path, unknownKeys(strictErr))
42			}
43			return nil, fmt.Errorf("config %s: %w", path, err)
44		}
45		if err := c.normalize(); err != nil {
46			return nil, fmt.Errorf("config %s: %w", path, err)
47		}
48		if err := c.validate(); err != nil {
49			return nil, fmt.Errorf("config %s: %w", path, err)
50		}
51		return &c, nil
52	}
53	
54	// unknownKeys renders a StrictMissingError's row list as a comma-separated
55	// list of dotted key paths (e.g. "wireguard.peers.nane"), so a misspelled
56	// TOML key surfaces as a precise, single-line diagnostic instead of the
57	// library's raw multiline dump.
58	func unknownKeys(err *toml.StrictMissingError) string {
59		keys := make([]string, 0, len(err.Errors))
60		for _, e := range err.Errors {
61			keys = append(keys, strings.Join(e.Key(), "."))
62		}
63		return strings.Join(keys, ", ")
```

```go
193	// callers.
194	func (s *ActiveBackup) Recompute() {
195		s.mu.Lock()
196		defer s.mu.Unlock()
197		s.recomputeLocked(s.clock.Now())

210	//     -> a failback candidate: hold on the current active until best has been
211	//     continuously up for FailbackAfter, THEN fail back. If the current active
212	//     itself goes down mid-dwell, move to best at once rather than stall.
213	func (s *ActiveBackup) recomputeLocked(now time.Time) {
214		best := s.bestEligibleLocked()
215	
216		if best < 0 {
217			s.pending = -1
218			s.setActiveLocked(-1, "no eligible path")
219			return
220		}
221		if s.active < 0 || best > s.active {
222			// Cold start, or the active path is no longer eligible (best, the first
223			// eligible index, sits below it) -> immediate (fail)over.
224			s.pending = -1
225			s.setActiveLocked(best, "failover")
226			return
227		}
228		if best == s.active {
229			s.pending = -1
230			return
231		}
232	
233		// best < s.active: a higher-priority path recovered -> debounced failback.
234		if s.pending != best {
235			s.pending = best
236			s.pendingSince = now
237		}
238		if now.Sub(s.pendingSince) >= s.cfg.FailbackAfter {
239			s.pending = -1
240			s.setActiveLocked(best, "failback")
241			return
242		}
243		// Still within the dwell: keep the current active while it stays eligible; if
244		// it has gone down, we cannot stall on it — move to best now.
245		if s.health[s.active].State() != telemetry.StateUp {
246			s.pending = -1
247			s.setActiveLocked(best, "failover during failback dwell")
248		}
249	}
250	
251	// bestEligibleLocked returns the lowest-index path reporting StateUp, or -1.
252	func (s *ActiveBackup) bestEligibleLocked() int {
253		for i := range s.health {
254			if s.health[i].State() == telemetry.StateUp {
255				return i
256			}
257		}
258		return -1
259	}
260	
261	// setActiveLocked stores the active index and logs a transition only when it
262	// actually changes, so a saturated send path does not log on every Pick.
263	func (s *ActiveBackup) setActiveLocked(idx int, reason string) {
264		if s.active == idx {
265			return
266		}
267		from := s.active
268		s.active = idx
269		s.log.Info("scheduler active path change",
270			"from", from,
271			"to", idx,
272			"reason", reason,
273		)
274	}
```

```go
1	package sched
2	
3	import (
4		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/telemetry"
5	)
6	
7	// Negative Pick sentinels. Pick returns a NON-NEGATIVE path index when a datagram
8	// should egress, and one of these when it should not. They are distinct so the Send
9	// path can tell a genuine outage (no eligible path) apart from a transient pacer
10	// shedding a frame while every path is healthy — the two must not read the same in
11	// operator logs or the e2e log-grep harness.
12	const (
13		// PickNone means no path is currently eligible (a total outage among this
14		// scheduler's paths). The Bind maps it to "no healthy path".
15		PickNone = -1
16		// PickPaced means eligible paths EXIST but every one is momentarily paced out, so
17		// this frame is shed (dropped) to bound egress and the send backlog. The paths are
18		// healthy — this is deliberate rate limiting, NOT an outage. Only a pacing-enabled
19		// weighted scheduler ever returns it.
20		PickPaced = -2
21	)
22	
23	// FrameClass tags an outbound datagram with the traffic class the pacer must treat
24	// it as (defect D22). The multipath Bind classifies each frame from the inner
25	// WireGuard message type and passes the class to Pick so a pacing scheduler can give
26	// WireGuard control frames (handshake init/response, cookie reply, keepalive) a
27	// different admission policy than bulk data. Pick() has no other frame-type
28	// visibility, so this is the ONLY channel by which frame-type reaches the pacer.
29	type FrameClass uint8
30	
31	const (
32		// ClassData is bulk/opaque data (a WireGuard transport datagram carrying tunnelled
33		// payload). It is fully paced: under a weighted scheduler with pacing enabled it is
34		// subject to the per-path token buckets and is shed (PickPaced) when they are empty.
35		ClassData FrameClass = iota
36		// ClassControl is a WireGuard control frame — handshake initiation/response, cookie
37		// reply, or keepalive. It is pacing-EXEMPT: a pacing scheduler never sheds it for an
38		// empty token bucket and never spends a data token for it, so sustained bulk overload
39		// cannot starve rekey (defect D22). Control frames originate from OUR OWN WireGuard
40		// engine on the send path (an attacker cannot inject them here), and WireGuard's own
41		// state machine bounds their rate, so exempting them from the data pace is safe.
42		ClassControl
43	)
44	
45	// Scheduler is the send-side path-selection policy the multipath Bind consults
46	// for every outbound datagram. It is the extension seam for the send scheduler:
47	// active-backup failover (ActiveBackup, the P1 MVP) is one implementation; the
48	// later weighted / FEC-aware policy (T21) is a DIFFERENT implementation of this
49	// same interface, swapped in at the composition root with no Bind change.
50	type Scheduler interface {
51		// Pick returns the index of the path the next datagram must egress on, in
52		// the path priority order the scheduler was built over (index 0 = preferred
53		// primary). It returns a negative value when no datagram should be sent this
54		// call; the negative value is one of the Pick* sentinels (PickNone for no
55		// eligible path, PickPaced for a healthy-but-paced-out shed), so the caller can
56		// tell a genuine outage apart from deliberate rate limiting. Pick is safe for
57		// concurrent callers on the Bind's send path.
58		//
59		// class is the frame's traffic class (defect D22): a pacing scheduler exempts
60		// ClassControl (WireGuard handshake/keepalive) from the data token buckets so
61		// bulk overload cannot shed control frames and starve rekey, while ClassData is
62		// fully paced. A non-pacing scheduler (active-backup) ignores class.
63		//
64		// Pick MAY be stateful: a weighted/aggregating scheduler advances its
65		// distribution bookkeeping (deficit/round-robin credits, pacing tokens, offered-
66		// load meter) on every call, so ONE Pick consumes ONE frame-selection slot.
67		// Callers that only want the liveness-derived active set refreshed — without
68		// perturbing distribution — MUST call Recompute, not Pick (see the T40 eager-
69		// failover nudge in the multipath Bind).
70		Pick(class FrameClass) int
71	
72		// Recompute refreshes the scheduler's liveness-derived selection state (which
73		// paths are eligible, which is the active primary) from the current PathHealth,
74		// and logs any active-path transition, WITHOUT selecting or consuming a frame
75		// slot: it advances no distribution/pacing/load state. It is the split-out
76		// "recompute the eligible/active set from liveness" half of the old Pick, so the
77		// eager-failover nudge (defect D18/T40) can drive an egress-lull failover recompute
78		// without stealing a weighted-distribution slot the way a spurious Pick would. For
79		// an idempotent scheduler (active-backup) it is exactly Pick without the return;
80		// for a stateful one (weighted) it is strictly the non-consuming subset. It is
81		// safe for concurrent callers and never calls back into the Bind.
82		Recompute()
83	}
84	
85	// PathQuality is the per-path measured-quality source the weighted scheduler reads
86	// to derive distribution weights: the read side of the T13 telemetry Estimate
87	// (RTT/Jitter/Loss), ANALOGOUS to PathHealth but exposing the quality snapshot
88	// rather than the up/down verdict. *telemetry.Prober satisfies BOTH PathHealth and
89	// PathQuality (its Estimate() is mutex-guarded, so it meets the same concurrency
90	// contract PathHealth documents), which is how one *Prober per path feeds liveness
91	// AND weight to the scheduler. The weighted scheduler's unit tests drive a synthetic
92	// PathQuality so the RTT/loss → weight formula is exercised on hand-built Estimates
93	// with no real probe stream.
94	type PathQuality interface {
95		Estimate() telemetry.Estimate
96	}
97	
98	// DynamicScheduler is a Scheduler whose path set can change at runtime (T30): a
99	// path may be admitted or dropped WITHOUT rebuilding the scheduler or disturbing
100	// the active selection of the surviving paths. The multipath Bind holds its
101	// scheduler as a plain Scheduler and type-asserts to this richer interface only
102	// when a runtime path add/remove is requested, so the Scheduler seam (and the
103	// T21 weighted policy that will implement it) is preserved: a scheduler that
104	// does not support dynamic membership simply cannot back a reloadable bind.
105	//
106	// The index space is the SAME priority-ordered index Pick returns, and the Bind
107	// keeps its own path slice index-aligned with this scheduler's path list, so an
108	// index returned by Pick always addresses the matching path. Both methods are
109	// safe for concurrent callers.
110	type DynamicScheduler interface {
111		Scheduler
112		// AddPath admits h as a NEW lowest-priority path (highest index) and returns
113		// that index. Appending at the tail cannot change any existing path's index
114		// nor steal the active selection from a higher-priority survivor: an
115		// active-backup policy only ever selects the new path once every
116		// higher-priority path is down. h must be non-nil.
117		AddPath(h PathHealth) (int, error)
118		// RemovePath drops the path at index i (shifting higher indices down by one)
119		// and repairs the cached selection so a surviving path keeps being selected
120		// and, if the dropped path was the active one, egress fails over to the best
121		// remaining path on the next Pick. i must be in range.
122		RemovePath(i int) error
123		// SetPaths replaces the ENTIRE path/health list with health (priority order,
124		// index 0 = preferred primary) and clears any cached selection, so the next
125		// Pick re-derives the active path from live liveness. The Bind calls it on
126		// every Open to re-align the scheduler's membership with the path slice it
127		// rebuilds from the current definitions — the single reconciliation point that
128		// makes a runtime path add/remove survive a Close→Open cycle index-aligned
129		// (T30). It is safe for concurrent callers; health must be non-empty and hold
130		// no nil element.
131		SetPaths(health []PathHealth) error
132	}
133	
134	// PathHealth is the per-path liveness the scheduler consumes: the read side of
135	// the T13 telemetry liveness state machine, so the scheduler reads exactly the
136	// up/down verdict T13 already computes (with its own probe-loss hysteresis)
137	// rather than duplicating detection here.
138	//
139	// Concurrency contract: Pick runs on the Bind send path CONCURRENTLY with the
140	// probe/timer goroutines that mutate liveness, so a production PathHealth MUST
141	// be INTERNALLY SYNCHRONIZED. *telemetry.Prober satisfies this — its State()
142	// takes the Prober's mutex (probe.go). A bare *telemetry.Liveness does NOT: it
143	// is not independently synchronized (probe.go: "must be reached only through
144	// the Prober") and its State() is an unguarded field read (liveness.go), so
145	// wiring a live, probe-driven *Liveness directly here is a data race — use a
146	// *Prober or an explicitly-locked adapter instead. (The sched unit tests drive
147	// a raw *Liveness on a single goroutine with no concurrent writer, which is not
148	// a race and is fine for testing.)
149	type PathHealth interface {
150		State() telemetry.PathState
151	}
152	
153	// AlwaysUp is a PathHealth that reports StateUp unconditionally. It is the
154	// composition-root placeholder used until the probe-transport wiring (a
155	// follow-up task) drives real per-path *telemetry.Prober liveness: with every
156	// path statically Up, the active-backup scheduler keeps all egress on the
157	// preferred primary (the data-thrift bring-up behaviour), and swapping in real
158	// Probers later activates failover with no further Bind or scheduler change.
159	type AlwaysUp struct{}
160	
161	// State reports the path as up.
162	func (AlwaysUp) State() telemetry.PathState { return telemetry.StateUp }
```

**`internal/config/load.go`** — Load(function), unknownKeys(function), requiredMode(constant)

```go
1	package config
2	
3	import (
4		"bytes"
5		"errors"
6		"fmt"
7		"io/fs"
8		"os"
9		"strings"
10	
11		"github.com/pelletier/go-toml/v2"
12	)
13	
14	// requiredMode is the exact permission bits the config file must carry. The file
15	// holds the WireGuard private key and the outer-control PSK, so it must not be
16	// readable by group or others.
17	const requiredMode fs.FileMode = 0o600
18	
19	// Load reads, validates, and returns the configuration at path. It fails fast:
20	// the file must have mode 0600 exactly, must be valid TOML, and must satisfy the
21	// required-field invariants.
22	func Load(path string) (*Config, error) {
23		info, err := os.Stat(path)
24		if err != nil {
25			return nil, fmt.Errorf("config: %w", err)
26		}
27		if perm := info.Mode().Perm(); perm != requiredMode {
28			return nil, fmt.Errorf("config %s: insecure permissions %#o, must be %#o (holds private key and PSK)", path, perm, requiredMode)
29		}
30	
31		data, err := os.ReadFile(path)
32		if err != nil {
33			return nil, fmt.Errorf("config: %w", err)
34		}
35	
36		var c Config
37		dec := toml.NewDecoder(bytes.NewReader(data)).DisallowUnknownFields()
38		if err := dec.Decode(&c); err != nil {
39			var strictErr *toml.StrictMissingError
40			if errors.As(err, &strictErr) {
41				return nil, fmt.Errorf("config %s: unknown key %s", path, unknownKeys(strictErr))
42			}
43			return nil, fmt.Errorf("config %s: %w", path, err)
44		}
45		if err := c.normalize(); err != nil {
46			return nil, fmt.Errorf("config %s: %w", path, err)
47		}
48		if err := c.validate(); err != nil {
49			return nil, fmt.Errorf("config %s: %w", path, err)
50		}
51		return &c, nil
52	}
53	
54	// unknownKeys renders a StrictMissingError's row list as a comma-separated
55	// list of dotted key paths (e.g. "wireguard.peers.nane"), so a misspelled
56	// TOML key surfaces as a precise, single-line diagnostic instead of the
57	// library's raw multiline dump.
58	func unknownKeys(err *toml.StrictMissingError) string {
59		keys := make([]string, 0, len(err.Errors))
60		for _, e := range err.Errors {
61			keys = append(keys, strings.Join(e.Key(), "."))
62		}
63		return strings.Join(keys, ", ")
64	}
```

```go
218			s.setActiveLocked(-1, "no eligible path")
219			return
220		}
221		if s.active < 0 || best > s.active {
222			// Cold start, or the active path is no longer eligible (best, the first
223			// eligible index, sits below it) -> immediate (fail)over.
224			s.pending = -1
225			s.setActiveLocked(best, "failover")
226			return
227		}
228		if best == s.active {
229			s.pending = -1
230			return
231		}
232	
233		// best < s.active: a higher-priority path recovered -> debounced failback.
234		if s.pending != best {
235			s.pending = best
236			s.pendingSince = now
237		}
238		if now.Sub(s.pendingSince) >= s.cfg.FailbackAfter {
239			s.pending = -1
240			s.setActiveLocked(best, "failback")
241			return
242		}
243		// Still within the dwell: keep the current active while it stays eligible; if
244		// it has gone down, we cannot stall on it — move to best now.
245		if s.health[s.active].State() != telemetry.StateUp {
246			s.pending = -1
247			s.setActiveLocked(best, "failover during failback dwell")
248		}
249	}
250	
251	// bestEligibleLocked returns the lowest-index path reporting StateUp, or -1.
252	func (s *ActiveBackup) bestEligibleLocked() int {
253		for i := range s.health {
254			if s.health[i].State() == telemetry.StateUp {
255				return i
256			}
257		}
258		return -1
259	}
260	
261	// setActiveLocked stores the active index and logs a transition only when it
262	// actually changes, so a saturated send path does not log on every Pick.
263	func (s *ActiveBackup) setActiveLocked(idx int, reason string) {
264		if s.active == idx {
265			return
266		}
267		from := s.active
268		s.active = idx
269		s.log.Info("scheduler active path change",
270			"from", from,
271			"to", idx,
272			"reason", reason,
273		)
274	}
275	
```

```go
10	// Field key conventions. Per-path events carry FieldPath so logs can be filtered
11	// by uplink; every component-scoped logger carries FieldComponent.
12	const (
13		FieldComponent = "component"
14		FieldPath      = "path"
15	)
16	
17	// Logger is the structured-logging surface the rest of wanbond depends on, so no
18	// package imports log/slog directly. It supports component- and path-scoped
19	// child loggers whose scope attributes are attached to every subsequent record.
20	type Logger interface {
21		Debug(msg string, args ...any)
22		Info(msg string, args ...any)
23		Warn(msg string, args ...any)
24		Error(msg string, args ...any)
25		// Component returns a child logger tagged with the given component name.
26		Component(name string) Logger
27		// Path returns a child logger tagged with the given path name.
28		Path(name string) Logger
29	}

31	// slogLogger is the slog-backed implementation of Logger.
32	type slogLogger struct {
33		l *slog.Logger
34	}
35	
36	// New builds a JSON-handler Logger writing to w at the given level. An unknown
37	// level is a configuration error (fail fast); an empty level defaults to info.
38	func New(level string, w io.Writer) (Logger, error) {
39		lvl, err := parseLevel(level)
40		if err != nil {
41			return nil, err
42		}
43		h := slog.NewJSONHandler(w, &slog.HandlerOptions{Level: lvl})
44		return &slogLogger{l: slog.New(h)}, nil
45	}
46	
47	func parseLevel(level string) (slog.Level, error) {
48		switch strings.ToLower(strings.TrimSpace(level)) {
49		case "", "info":
50			return slog.LevelInfo, nil
51		case "debug":
52			return slog.LevelDebug, nil
53		case "warn", "warning":
54			return slog.LevelWarn, nil
55		case "error":
56			return slog.LevelError, nil
57		default:
58			return 0, fmt.Errorf("log: unknown level %q (want debug|info|warn|error)", level)
59		}
60	}
61	
62	func (s *slogLogger) Debug(msg string, args ...any) { s.l.Debug(msg, args...) }
63	func (s *slogLogger) Info(msg string, args ...any)  { s.l.Info(msg, args...) }
64	func (s *slogLogger) Warn(msg string, args ...any)  { s.l.Warn(msg, args...) }
65	func (s *slogLogger) Error(msg string, args ...any) { s.l.Error(msg, args...) }
66	
67	func (s *slogLogger) Component(name string) Logger {
68		return &slogLogger{l: s.l.With(FieldComponent, name)}
69	}
70	
71	func (s *slogLogger) Path(name string) Logger {
72		return &slogLogger{l: s.l.With(FieldPath, name)}
```

**`internal/sched/scheduler.go`** — Scheduler(interface), DynamicScheduler(interface), Recompute(method), Pick(method)

```go
1	package sched
2	
3	import (
4		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/telemetry"
5	)
6	
7	// Negative Pick sentinels. Pick returns a NON-NEGATIVE path index when a datagram
8	// should egress, and one of these when it should not. They are distinct so the Send
9	// path can tell a genuine outage (no eligible path) apart from a transient pacer
10	// shedding a frame while every path is healthy — the two must not read the same in
11	// operator logs or the e2e log-grep harness.
12	const (
13		// PickNone means no path is currently eligible (a total outage among this
14		// scheduler's paths). The Bind maps it to "no healthy path".
15		PickNone = -1
16		// PickPaced means eligible paths EXIST but every one is momentarily paced out, so
17		// this frame is shed (dropped) to bound egress and the send backlog. The paths are
18		// healthy — this is deliberate rate limiting, NOT an outage. Only a pacing-enabled
19		// weighted scheduler ever returns it.
20		PickPaced = -2
21	)
22	
23	// FrameClass tags an outbound datagram with the traffic class the pacer must treat
24	// it as (defect D22). The multipath Bind classifies each frame from the inner
25	// WireGuard message type and passes the class to Pick so a pacing scheduler can give
26	// WireGuard control frames (handshake init/response, cookie reply, keepalive) a
27	// different admission policy than bulk data. Pick() has no other frame-type
28	// visibility, so this is the ONLY channel by which frame-type reaches the pacer.
29	type FrameClass uint8
30	
31	const (
32		// ClassData is bulk/opaque data (a WireGuard transport datagram carrying tunnelled
33		// payload). It is fully paced: under a weighted scheduler with pacing enabled it is
34		// subject to the per-path token buckets and is shed (PickPaced) when they are empty.
35		ClassData FrameClass = iota
36		// ClassControl is a WireGuard control frame — handshake initiation/response, cookie
37		// reply, or keepalive. It is pacing-EXEMPT: a pacing scheduler never sheds it for an
38		// empty token bucket and never spends a data token for it, so sustained bulk overload
39		// cannot starve rekey (defect D22). Control frames originate from OUR OWN WireGuard
40		// engine on the send path (an attacker cannot inject them here), and WireGuard's own
41		// state machine bounds their rate, so exempting them from the data pace is safe.
42		ClassControl
43	)
44	
45	// Scheduler is the send-side path-selection policy the multipath Bind consults
46	// for every outbound datagram. It is the extension seam for the send scheduler:
47	// active-backup failover (ActiveBackup, the P1 MVP) is one implementation; the
48	// later weighted / FEC-aware policy (T21) is a DIFFERENT implementation of this
49	// same interface, swapped in at the composition root with no Bind change.
50	type Scheduler interface {
51		// Pick returns the index of the path the next datagram must egress on, in
52		// the path priority order the scheduler was built over (index 0 = preferred
53		// primary). It returns a negative value when no datagram should be sent this
54		// call; the negative value is one of the Pick* sentinels (PickNone for no
55		// eligible path, PickPaced for a healthy-but-paced-out shed), so the caller can
56		// tell a genuine outage apart from deliberate rate limiting. Pick is safe for
57		// concurrent callers on the Bind's send path.
58		//
59		// class is the frame's traffic class (defect D22): a pacing scheduler exempts
60		// ClassControl (WireGuard handshake/keepalive) from the data token buckets so
61		// bulk overload cannot shed control frames and starve rekey, while ClassData is
62		// fully paced. A non-pacing scheduler (active-backup) ignores class.
63		//
64		// Pick MAY be stateful: a weighted/aggregating scheduler advances its
65		// distribution bookkeeping (deficit/round-robin credits, pacing tokens, offered-
66		// load meter) on every call, so ONE Pick consumes ONE frame-selection slot.
67		// Callers that only want the liveness-derived active set refreshed — without
68		// perturbing distribution — MUST call Recompute, not Pick (see the T40 eager-
69		// failover nudge in the multipath Bind).
70		Pick(class FrameClass) int
71	
72		// Recompute refreshes the scheduler's liveness-derived selection state (which
73		// paths are eligible, which is the active primary) from the current PathHealth,
74		// and logs any active-path transition, WITHOUT selecting or consuming a frame
75		// slot: it advances no distribution/pacing/load state. It is the split-out
76		// "recompute the eligible/active set from liveness" half of the old Pick, so the
77		// eager-failover nudge (defect D18/T40) can drive an egress-lull failover recompute
78		// without stealing a weighted-distribution slot the way a spurious Pick would. For
79		// an idempotent scheduler (active-backup) it is exactly Pick without the return;
80		// for a stateful one (weighted) it is strictly the non-consuming subset. It is
81		// safe for concurrent callers and never calls back into the Bind.
82		Recompute()
83	}
84	
85	// PathQuality is the per-path measured-quality source the weighted scheduler reads
86	// to derive distribution weights: the read side of the T13 telemetry Estimate
87	// (RTT/Jitter/Loss), ANALOGOUS to PathHealth but exposing the quality snapshot
88	// rather than the up/down verdict. *telemetry.Prober satisfies BOTH PathHealth and
89	// PathQuality (its Estimate() is mutex-guarded, so it meets the same concurrency
90	// contract PathHealth documents), which is how one *Prober per path feeds liveness
91	// AND weight to the scheduler. The weighted scheduler's unit tests drive a synthetic
92	// PathQuality so the RTT/loss → weight formula is exercised on hand-built Estimates
93	// with no real probe stream.
94	type PathQuality interface {
95		Estimate() telemetry.Estimate
96	}
97	
98	// DynamicScheduler is a Scheduler whose path set can change at runtime (T30): a
99	// path may be admitted or dropped WITHOUT rebuilding the scheduler or disturbing
100	// the active selection of the surviving paths. The multipath Bind holds its
101	// scheduler as a plain Scheduler and type-asserts to this richer interface only
102	// when a runtime path add/remove is requested, so the Scheduler seam (and the
103	// T21 weighted policy that will implement it) is preserved: a scheduler that
104	// does not support dynamic membership simply cannot back a reloadable bind.
105	//
106	// The index space is the SAME priority-ordered index Pick returns, and the Bind
107	// keeps its own path slice index-aligned with this scheduler's path list, so an
108	// index returned by Pick always addresses the matching path. Both methods are
109	// safe for concurrent callers.
110	type DynamicScheduler interface {
111		Scheduler
112		// AddPath admits h as a NEW lowest-priority path (highest index) and returns
113		// that index. Appending at the tail cannot change any existing path's index
114		// nor steal the active selection from a higher-priority survivor: an
115		// active-backup policy only ever selects the new path once every
116		// higher-priority path is down. h must be non-nil.
117		AddPath(h PathHealth) (int, error)
118		// RemovePath drops the path at index i (shifting higher indices down by one)
119		// and repairs the cached selection so a surviving path keeps being selected
120		// and, if the dropped path was the active one, egress fails over to the best
121		// remaining path on the next Pick. i must be in range.
122		RemovePath(i int) error
123		// SetPaths replaces the ENTIRE path/health list with health (priority order,
124		// index 0 = preferred primary) and clears any cached selection, so the next
125		// Pick re-derives the active path from live liveness. The Bind calls it on
126		// every Open to re-align the scheduler's membership with the path slice it
127		// rebuilds from the current definitions — the single reconciliation point that
128		// makes a runtime path add/remove survive a Close→Open cycle index-aligned
129		// (T30). It is safe for concurrent callers; health must be non-empty and hold
130		// no nil element.
131		SetPaths(health []PathHealth) error
132	}
133	
134	// PathHealth is the per-path liveness the scheduler consumes: the read side of
135	// the T13 telemetry liveness state machine, so the scheduler reads exactly the
136	// up/down verdict T13 already computes (with its own probe-loss hysteresis)
137	// rather than duplicating detection here.
138	//
139	// Concurrency contract: Pick runs on the Bind send path CONCURRENTLY with the
140	// probe/timer goroutines that mutate liveness, so a production PathHealth MUST
141	// be INTERNALLY SYNCHRONIZED. *telemetry.Prober satisfies this — its State()
142	// takes the Prober's mutex (probe.go). A bare *telemetry.Liveness does NOT: it
143	// is not independently synchronized (probe.go: "must be reached only through
144	// the Prober") and its State() is an unguarded field read (liveness.go), so
145	// wiring a live, probe-driven *Liveness directly here is a data race — use a
146	// *Prober or an explicitly-locked adapter instead. (The sched unit tests drive
147	// a raw *Liveness on a single goroutine with no concurrent writer, which is not
148	// a race and is fine for testing.)
149	type PathHealth interface {
150		State() telemetry.PathState
151	}
152	
153	// AlwaysUp is a PathHealth that reports StateUp unconditionally. It is the
154	// composition-root placeholder used until the probe-transport wiring (a
155	// follow-up task) drives real per-path *telemetry.Prober liveness: with every
156	// path statically Up, the active-backup scheduler keeps all egress on the
157	// preferred primary (the data-thrift bring-up behaviour), and swapping in real
158	// Probers later activates failover with no further Bind or scheduler change.
159	type AlwaysUp struct{}
160	
161	// State reports the path as up.
162	func (AlwaysUp) State() telemetry.PathState { return telemetry.StateUp }
```
